paragraphs.body: Second Excerpt

Context for the reader: I decided to make the narrator a woman instead of a man because I was having trouble getting into the mindset of the last one. The story is now told in present tense from Enid’s mom, Charlotte, who feels trapped in a marriage working multiple jobs and taking care of her daughter Enid because her husband doesn’t do anything. The following excerpt is a new beginning to the story that will mostly follow the same plot afterwards with some slight altercations. So far the mom, Charlotte finds the doll in the attic that her grandmother, Etna gave Enid who becomes obsessively attached to it.
Questions to answer:
Do we like the choice for a new narrator?
Is the switch to present tense effective in immersing the reader in the story?
When I bounce back and forth in time is it clear or confusing?
How is the opening description of the doll? Do her features need more explaining or imagery?
Anything else?
The Picture of Enid—new beginning excerpt

The rungs of the old latter groan as I unfold it from the trap door in the ceiling and turn on the light to the attic. It protests my weight as I climb, feeling the rough grain that had spent the past five years collecting dust generously sharing its treasure with my hands.

I stand up, ducking my head under nails from the roof and wipe my palms on my jeans. There are no windows in the attic, but a naked lightbulb exposes the neglected stash of Christmas lights that John never put up last year—despite his mother’s many snide remarks to me under her breath. I shuffle past the lights and a couple piles of boxes from when we moved in until I find what I’m looking for: an old chest with a rounded lid that caricaturizes a fullness even when it’s closed. The chest had been passed down to me by my mother, and now stores nostalgic clutter like my old prom dresses, Enid’s first pair of shoes, and my high school yearbooks—the import of my current venture.

annotations.comment: Very effective way of introducing both of these characters.

paragraphs.body: I let out a breath that antagonizes a thick layer of dust on the lid; it flies into the air provoking my eyes and working my nostrils into a violent sneeze. “Jesus,” I whisper to myself, rubbing my eyes and lifting the lid.
“MMMMMHHH!” I shriek through my nose, feeling my ass hit the floorboards as I fall backwards.

annotations.comment: Tiny note that it usually works better to write something like “I screamed” rather than trying to spell out what was screamed.

paragraphs.body: There’s a pair of painted blue eyes staring back at me from the open trunk. They clearly belong to an old-fashioned porcelain doll propped up against the wall of the trunk. Someone has stuck the doll facing forwards and standing against the front panel of the chest so that her eyes are flush and parallel to the seam of the base and lid. It looks like a miniature person climbing to sneak a peek over a neighbor’s fence.

Regaining myself, I peer back into the trunk. It isn’t that frightening at all; I’m surprised at my over-the-top reaction. Only, I hadn’t recalled ever putting the doll here. I recognized her, she belonged to John’s mother, Etna, as a child, who ceremoniously gave the doll to me on Enid’s first birthday.

“Charlotte,” she’d said, pausing from taking pictures of John holding Enid for her Facebook, “I have something to give to Enid, it’s very important to me, and it’s been in our family since my mother was a young girl. I expect you’ll take good care of it and make sure Enid enjoys it properly when she’s older. John never had any sisters…”
I started to zone her out; it had been a long day of house-decorating and cake-making, I was about ready for the party to be over, and it was only three o’clock—party started at two.
“Okay,” I said, cutting her off with a forced smile, “I’d be glad to hold onto the doll for her, thank you, Etna.”

annotations.comment: I read this before, but am still enjoying it.

paragraphs.body: She huffed to herself, but faithfully retrieved the doll from her car. She’d had it displayed in a cardboard box with a plastic window on the top to allow for full admiration of the thing.
“I’ve had her polished and boxed up. Just like new,” she said.

I almost felt warm towards the woman, touched that she would pass on her little treasure to Enid until she followed it up with, “you really should do something about this awful furniture. It’s bad enough you have John living in a swamp of green, but you have a baby now, you might consider something homier for the family.”
I’d found the set on Facebook marketplace for a great price from a couple that was moving. I wasn’t sure if she had a problem with the couch, or if she wanted an excuse to harp on me.
“I’ll put the doll somewhere safe,” I say in return.

Then, I remember putting the doll in the attic—but not in this chest; it was in Enid’s box. I make my way over there now and unfold the flaps to look inside. The doll’s box is still tucked between old clothes, but it’s empty.

I turn back towards my trunk and consider the doll. Her gaze meets mine over the edge of the wood. I can’t imagine a single way for her to have gotten there unless John had moved her, or if I had placed her there and forgotten. It seems like an odd thing to do either way. Moving closer, I pick her up to inspect more closely. Her hair is auburn and perfectly curled in stiff ringlets that go a little past her shoulders. Beneath her painted eyes is a puckered mouth and rosy cheeks that make the expression resemble somewhat a decorative ceramic wall mask.

annotations.comment: I enjoy that she is truly puzzled by this. In a lot of books she would simply move on, so I liked that she knows this is strange.

paragraphs.body: Oh well.

I set the doll next to me and rummage through the trunk until I find the neat stack of yearbooks spanning my elementary years to graduation. I slip a thin volume from 2000 out of the pile and flip to P.

I find my maiden name Perkins, a gap toothed 7-year-old grins widely into the camera above my printed name, staring at me. Her smile reaches past her eyes in the photo until she’s left with nothing but squinted skin where they’re meant to be.

I pull out my phone and snap a steady photo of my younger self for Enid’s school project; she’s meant to glue her own photo next to those of her parents when they were her age. My eyes drift to the top of the stack, 2011, the year I graduated. Struck by a nostalgic curiosity, I flip it open and take my time thumbing through the pages. My senior quote, half an attempt at being funny, half honest reads “was released from her four-year sentence.” I wanted it to follow my name, but the yearbook committee put our senior photos in between our names and our quotes. I flip past my gently smiling brunette self, with no lines furrowing between her brow, no premature grays straggling through her smooth hairline, until I get to the senior Q/A’s.

annotations.comment: Again, this feels very realistic — what a typical project.

paragraphs.body: Where do you see yourself in 10 years?
I don’t know, hopefully I’ll be finishing up medical school or I’ll already be a cardiologist. Maybe I’ll be married, who knows?

annotations.comment: I like the little shock this must have caused, but probably need a little more to get that across.

paragraphs.body: I close the book and stack it back with the others, storing them in their corner of the chest and close the trunk. The porcelain doll is still staring at me from her supine position on the floorboards; I grab her but leave the box that she came in stored with Enid’s other things.
Downstairs, John is playing another shooter-POV game on the TV, I can hear the blasting sound effects as I descend the staircase. He doesn’t hear me the first time I call his name.
“John!” I say louder raising my arms—doll in hand—and dropping them.
“What, what! What do you want I’m busy.” He leans further into his game as if closing the distance will somehow make him better at it.
I reach for the remote as if to shut it off and he immediately pauses it.
“Charlotte, what the actual fuck are you doing,” he snarls.

annotations.comment: An immediate sense that these two are absolutely not okay.
annotations.context_right: actual fuck are you doing,” he snarls.

paragraphs.body: I try to swallow the anger that bubbles into my throat. “John,” I say curtly, “where is Enid?”
“She’s playing outside with Squint; I think she said she was going to take him for a walk.”

“What do you mean?” I almost shout, “John she’s seven you need to be watching her! I left her with you for twenty minutes; you’re telling me you couldn’t put your goddamn game down for twenty minutes?”
“Calm the fuck down, Charlotte. I’ve had a long week, it’s Saturday. She’ll be fine Squint is a big dog.”
“Oh, you had a long week?” I shouted back accusatorily, my voice threatening to break. “You,” I spit, “who has been out of work for a month and has yet to hear back from a single job?”

annotations.comment: The dialogue here felt a little on the nose (but was good to this point).

paragraphs.body: I take a deep breath to ground myself.
“I’ve been applying to places all week! It’s not helping that you’re being so hard on me, do you think I like being in this position? Do you think it’s easy for me?”
I glance at the TV and then back to his unwashed, food-stained sweatpants. He hadn’t done his laundry all week even though I’d told him I didn’t have time to do it this week.

I don’t respond; instead, I take a deep breath in the name of all that is holy and walk out the back door to find my daughter. She’s sitting under the oak tree in the backyard with Squint who’s taken the opportunity to lie down in the shade on the hot September day.

Our old bulldog, Squint, so named for the folds around his eyes, lifts a heavy head when I open the door, but Enid doesn’t notice me yet. I heave a deep sigh of relief that she opted to sit and read him a picture book instead of trying to take him on a walk. I relax a little and let myself enjoy the picture in front of me. It’s all for her, I’m doing it all for her.

annotations.text: It’s all for her, I’m doing it all for her.
annotations.comment: This really hit home. I love how you’ve created a world that’s interesting *without* any metaphysical or horror elements.

paragraphs.body: “Enid, honey, whatcha up to?” I ask her playfully.
“I’m reading to Squint, Mrs. Cierra gave us books to practice reading this weekend, mine’s The Giving Tree.”
How very fitting, I laugh at the universe to myself. “That’s a classic, honey. Mommy was reading that when she was your age, too.”
“Really?” she perks up, eyebrows jumping towards her hairline.

“Yes, really. Why don’t you read it to me and Squint and maybe we can take him on a walk before I go to work tonight.” I check my watch, 1:00pm. Etna should be here in a couple hours to watch Enid for the night while I’m at work. The dinner shift at The Clubhouse starts at 4:00.

annotations.comment: Okay, this is a good way to bring in her job. I will say, unless she works at a very fancy restaurant, supporting a family of three on a waitress salary must be very stressful, maybe even more than you show here.

paragraphs.body: Enid doesn’t reply, I follow her gaze to the doll in my hand that I’d forgotten about.
“What’s that?”
“Oh… it’s from Grandma Etna,” I say, timing my response, “she gave it to you when you were just one year old! I’ve been keeping it safe for you this whole time.”
“Really?” she squeals, her whole face lights up and she rushes to grab the doll.

I don’t let go immediately, “Okay, Enid,” I explain, “she’s very fragile and very old so if I give her to you, you have to promise to take extra good care of her. She’s older than me and Grandma combined, so she needs you to be very gentle with her.”
“I promise I will be! I pinky promise! I promise! Can I please have her?”

I relinquish the doll to her eager clutches, and she immediately holds it to her chest. It’s a strange picture, this seven-year-old wearing a Bluey t-shirt holding on to an Edwardian-looking porcelain doll like it’s just changed her life. I didn’t think she would react this strongly to it.

annotations.context_right: her eager clutches, and she immediately holds it to her chest. It’s a strange picture, this seven-y

paragraphs.body: “Let’s take Squint for a walk,” I say, “you can play with the doll later.”
“No! She should come with us!”
I’m too tired to struggle with her so I give in.

Enid loves taking Squint for walks, because it means we’ll stop at Mrs. Winkly’s house and visit her little hamster, or on most days, giggle and watch it run around Mrs. Winkly’s living room floor in the lime-green plastic bubble that grants the creature, what they consider, freedom.

Today is no different, Enid rings the doorbell and is greeted by a smiling middle-aged woman with short brown hair and an unusually—bless her—high tolerance for other people’s children, herself being a preschool teacher during the week.
“Hi, Mrs. Winkly!” Enid says excitedly, and before the woman can respond she asks, “is Biscuit home?”
“As a matter of fact, he is,” replies Mrs. Winkly, “and I think Kaylie has him in his exercise ball right now, why don’t you go in and see.”

Enid squeals. “BISCUIT,” she calls and charges into their living room; I stop to greet Mrs. Winkly with common niceties, thanking her for her forbearance and hospitality. I then tie Squint to a tree out front, who quickly takes advantage of the opportunity to lie down in the shade.

When I walk inside, Enid and Kaylie are already hovering over the little hamster as he shoots around the carpet in every direction, helpless to escape them. His small nails make frantic scratching sounds on the plastic. It’s hard for me to imagine the hamster enjoying this ‘freedom’ presumed to be given by the green plastic ball, a victim much less of footfall, and more of a constant ogling; he cannot outrun their gaze. Enid is no different, she chases the little thing all over the Winkly’s living room floor and kitchen, entranced and erupting unrhythmically with mesmerized giggles.

annotations.comment: I love the implicit comparison to her own life here.

paragraphs.body: We usually only stay for ten, fifteen minutes at most until I tell her it’s probably time to get out of Mrs. Winkly’s hair, but when I look back at Enid, I notice her face has gone completely still, concentrating all of her energy into tracking Biscuit. She wears a thoughtless smile that seems stuck on her face without her knowledge. It’s like the muscles forgot they were working; meanwhile her eyes have gone wide and focused as they follow Biscuit’s path. The result is a creepy, incongruous, mask-like expression. I look at the doll clutched against her hip; she looks back at me.

annotations.comment: Maybe mention that this is an expression she has never seen before?
annotations.context_left: We usually only stay for ten, fifteen minutes at most until I tell her it’s probably ti
annotations.context_right: to get out of Mrs. Winkly’s hair, but when I look back at Enid, I notice her face has gone complete

paragraphs.body: “Alright, Enid say goodbye to Biscuit and Mrs. Winkly, it’s time to go home”
Enid snaps back into herself. “Oh, but I don’t want to go! Just five more minutes, please!”
“Oh, there’s no need to be in such a rush,” says Mrs. Winkly, “It’s always a delight to see Enid, and I know Biscuit looks forward to seeing her, too.”

I look at Biscuit. He had run so hard into the couch that the lime-green plastic bubble had gotten wedged in between the couch and the floor. His nails continue to make light scratching sounds against the side of the plastic as he throws his bodyweight in different directions, but to no avail.
“That’s alright, it’s still hot out, I don’t want Squint to overheat,” I say, “Enid, tell Mrs. Winkly goodbye, you’ll see her again tomorrow.”
“Goodbye, Mrs. Winkly, Goodbye Kaylie, goodbye Biscuit!” She stoops to wave to the hamster as Mrs. Winkly gently tugs his ball out of the couch.
“See ya later, Alligator!”
“Bye Enid!”

The corners of her mouth drop at tad as she waves goodbye to the little gerbil, but her expression perks back up at the sight of Squint, she gives him a few thoughtful strokes between his hears as her leans out to sniff her.

annotations.comment: I wanted maybe one more weird thing in this interaction — perhaps some mention of the doll by Mrs. Winkly and/or Kaylie, or something. All of that said, Grace, I think your classmates are going to go NUTS for this revision and rightly so — it’s so beautifully done. These people feel real, I really felt for your main character, and I’m full of dread for what might be coming. Keep writing! You’ve hit the flow.
